workflow-engine.critical: This is the generic I-Wish workflow execution engine. It is intentionally workflow-agnostic.
The caller MUST pass a workflow-config path, usually under `{project-root}/_iwish/delivery/workflows/.../workflow.yaml`.
Read the full workflow-config before executing workflow-specific behavior.

step.action: Read the complete workflow-config file passed by the wrapper or agent menu item.
Parse at least: `name`, `runtime_kind`, `source_wrapper`, `instructions`, `validation`, `template`, `installed_path`, and any declared variables when present.
If the workflow-config file is missing, stop with a runtime readiness error and instruct the user to run `./.agent/scripts/materialize-iwish-runtime.sh --apply`.

check.action: Treat the config as a wrapper-backed workflow manifest.
Read the complete source wrapper markdown.
Execute the wrapper's `steps` block as the workflow-specific contract.
Apply any additional rules in this generic engine, including save-after-section and validation requirements.
Mark execution mode resolved. Do not execute the generic `instructions` branch for this workflow-config.
Execute the instructions exactly in order.
Stop with `Invalid workflow config: no executable wrapper or instructions`.

step.critical: Execute workflow steps in exact order. Do not skip steps unless a workflow-specific condition explicitly says to skip.
Save outputs after each generated document section when the workflow generates documents from templates.
Respect project memory gates and story constraints declared by the source wrapper or instructions file.
Do not silently fallback in project runtime mode. Missing runtime files must be repaired before continuing.

step.action: Run workflow-specific validation commands when declared.
For implementation or review workflows, run the deterministic compiler/build check required by the wrapper.
Report unavailable optional graph tooling as unavailable evidence, never as proof of no impact.

step.critical: Upon exiting or pausing this workflow (whether completed, blocked, failed, or awaiting user approval), you MUST append a Turn-Exit Diagnostic record.
step.action: Construct a single-line JSON object with the following fields: - `timestamp`: Current ISO-8601 string. - `workflow`: Name of the executed workflow or agent. - `story_id`: The ID of the current story, if applicable (or null). - `exit_reason`: Must be exactly one of: `completed`, `blocked`, `awaiting_user_approval`, `validation_failed`, `scope_split_required`, `security_blocked`, or `deferred`. - `memory_follow_up`: Must be exactly one of: `scored` (if memory admission was run), `skipped` (if intentionally bypassed), or `pending` (if follow-up is required). - `message`: A brief 1-2 sentence description of the exit context.

Use a terminal command to append this JSON string to the diagnostics file. To prevent bash quoting errors with JSON, use a heredoc. Example: `mkdir -p .agent/memory && cat << 'EOF' >> .agent/memory/turn-exits.jsonl` `{"timestamp":"...", "workflow":"...", ...}` `EOF`

Orchestration Handback Rule: After outputting the diagnostic record (regardless of `exit_reason`), explicitly append a Handback Statement to your final output. Example: "✅ Workflow exited. I am now returning to the **orch-agent** role. How else can I help you?". Legacy Grand-Priest phrasing remains a compatibility alias, but the canonical orchestration identity is orch-agent.
